STUDY_DATA.title: Cloud Fundamentals
concepts: 6 Advantages of Cloud: Trade CAPEX→OPEX, Economies of Scale, Stop Guessing Capacity, Speed & Agility, Stop Running Data Centers, Go Global in Minutes
CAPEX = Capital Expenditure (upfront hardware). OPEX = Operational Expenditure (pay-as-you-go). Cloud shifts CAPEX to OPEX
Elasticity = auto scale UP/DOWN for short-term demand spikes (e.g. Black Friday). Scalability = handle growing long-term load
High Availability = minimize downtime with brief failover acceptable. Fault Tolerance = zero downtime, zero data loss (more redundant)
NIST 5 Characteristics: On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service
Agility = deploy/experiment in minutes instead of weeks → reduces time-to-market significantly
Economies of Scale: AWS buys hardware in massive bulk → passes savings to customers as lower prices over time
traps: Elasticity ≠ Scalability. Elasticity = dynamic short-term auto-scaling. Scalability = planned long-term capacity growth
Agility = speed to deploy resources, NOT automatic scaling (that is Elasticity)
Cloud does NOT eliminate ALL costs — OPEX still exists (pay per use). It eliminates CAPEX (upfront)
On-Demand Self-Service = provision resources WITHOUT human approval. NOT the same as auto-scaling

STUDY_DATA.title: Global Infrastructure
concepts: Region: Geographic area with minimum 3 isolated Availability Zones (e.g., ap-southeast-1 = Singapore)
Availability Zone (AZ): One or more discrete data centers with independent power, cooling & networking within a Region
AZ interconnect: Connected via <2ms high-bandwidth private fiber → enables Synchronous Replication between AZs
Edge Location: 400+ CDN cache points globally. Used by CloudFront (CDN) and Route 53 (DNS) to reduce user latency
Data Sovereignty: Data NEVER leaves a Region unless YOU explicitly configure cross-region replication
Region Selection Criteria: 1) Compliance/Data Residency laws, 2) Latency to end users, 3) AWS service availability, 4) Price
Local Zones: AWS infra extension into metro areas for ultra-low latency without full Region overhead
AWS Outposts: AWS hardware/rack installed ON-PREMISES. Run native AWS services inside your own data center
Global Services (no region): IAM, CloudFront, Route 53, WAF, Trusted Advisor, Organizations
traps: Edge Locations ≠ Availability Zones. Edge = CDN cache for CloudFront. AZ = full compute + storage data center
AZ ≠ a single building. An AZ can comprise multiple buildings within the same fault isolation boundary
Multi-AZ = protects against a single data center failure. Multi-Region = protects against full Region-wide outage
Synchronous replication = only feasible within same Region (low latency). Cross-Region replication is ASYNC

STUDY_DATA.title: Service Models
concepts: On-Premises: YOU own and manage EVERYTHING — rack, network, OS, runtime, apps, data
IaaS (e.g., EC2): AWS: Physical + Hypervisor. YOU manage: OS, Runtime, Middleware, Apps, Data
PaaS (e.g., Elastic Beanstalk, RDS): AWS: up to Runtime. YOU manage: App code + Data only
SaaS (e.g., WorkMail, Chime, Salesforce): AWS manages everything. YOU: just use the software
FaaS/Serverless (Lambda): AWS manages infra + runtime. YOU: write function code, triggered by events
Responsibility boundary: Shifts upward (toward app layer) as you move IaaS → PaaS → SaaS
Elastic Beanstalk: PaaS — auto-provisions EC2, ASG, ELB, RDS from app code. You still can SSH into EC2
Lambda execution model: Event → Lambda invoked → code runs → billed per ms + invocation count
traps: IaaS = EC2 (NOT PaaS). PaaS = Elastic Beanstalk, RDS. SaaS = WorkMail, Chime
In SaaS, customer still owns their DATA — AWS doesn't own your business data
Lambda is FaaS (subset of Serverless/PaaS), NOT SaaS. You still write and own the code
Elastic Beanstalk is PaaS — it provisions EC2 under the hood. Guest OS patches = AWS responsibility here

STUDY_DATA.title: Deployment Models
concepts: Public Cloud: All infra on AWS. No physical data center overhead. Best: startups, variable unpredictable workloads
Private Cloud (On-Premises): Full physical control. High CAPEX. Best: stable long-term workloads, strict compliance/data sovereignty
Hybrid Cloud: Sensitive/regulated data on-prem INTEGRATED with burst compute or web-facing services in cloud
Cloud-Native: Architectures fully designed for cloud — microservices, auto-scaling, managed services, event-driven
When to choose Cloud: Unpredictable/seasonal load, no existing DC, small IT team, fast time-to-market priority
When to choose On-Prem: Workload stable 5+ years, large existing infra investment, strict data residency laws
When to choose Hybrid: Regulated data must stay on-prem + need cloud burst capacity or global content delivery
AWS Outposts: Hybrid implementation — AWS manages the rack on-premises, you consume AWS services locally
traps: Hybrid ≠ just using both cloud and on-prem independently. They must INTEGRATE and communicate as one architecture
Moving fully to On-Prem from Cloud: gain full control + compliance. Lose: elasticity, global reach, economies of scale
On-Prem CAN be cost-effective for very stable, predictable, long-running workloads (no elasticity needed)

STUDY_DATA.title: Shared Responsibility
concepts: AWS responsible "OF the Cloud": Physical hardware, data center facilities, AZ networking, hypervisor, managed services below customer OS
Customer responsible "IN the Cloud": Guest OS patching, app code, IAM policies, data encryption, Security Groups (firewall rules)
EC2 (IaaS): YOU patch the Guest OS. YOU configure Security Groups inbound/outbound. YOU manage AMI
RDS (Managed PaaS): AWS patches the DB engine & underlying OS. YOU manage data, schemas, access control
Lambda (FaaS/Serverless): AWS manages the entire runtime environment. YOU write the function code only
Shared domain — Encryption: Customer CONFIGURES encryption settings. AWS provides KMS Key Management infrastructure
Physical security 100% AWS: Data center guards, cameras, disk disposal, hardware decommission
IAM User/Role management: ALWAYS customer responsibility regardless of service model
traps: Guest OS on EC2 = CUSTOMER must patch/update. Windows Updates, Linux yum updates = your job
Network devices inside AWS data centers (routers, switches) = 100% AWS responsibility
In PaaS/SaaS, OS patching shifts to AWS — but DATA SECURITY always remains customer's responsibility
Physical disk disposal after decommission = AWS responsibility. Logical data deletion = Customer

STUDY_DATA.title: Amazon EC2
concepts: EC2 = IaaS: Resizable virtual servers (instances). Customer manages everything from Guest OS upward
General Purpose (t, m): Balanced CPU/RAM ratio. Ideal: web servers, small/medium apps, dev environments
Compute Optimized (c): High vCPU-to-memory ratio. Ideal: batch processing, HPC, ML inference, gaming
Memory Optimized (r, x, z): Very high RAM. Ideal: in-memory DBs (Redis), SAP HANA, Hadoop, Spark
Storage Optimized (i, d, h): Very high sequential disk I/O. Ideal: OLTP, Cassandra, data warehouses
Accelerated Computing (p, g, inf): GPU or custom ASIC chips. Ideal: ML training, video encoding, graphics
On-Demand pricing: Pay per second (Linux/Ubuntu) or per hour (Windows). No commitment. Default choice
Reserved Instances (RI): 1 or 3 year term. Up to 72% savings. Standard (fixed) or Convertible (can change)
Spot Instances: Use spare AWS capacity. Up to 90% savings. CAN BE INTERRUPTED with 2-min notice
Dedicated Host: Entire physical server allocated to you. BYOL software licenses. Compliance (single-tenant)
Savings Plans: Compute SP (flexible: EC2, Lambda, Fargate) or EC2 Instance SP (fixed family, more discount)
traps: EC2 is NOT serverless — you manage the OS, and you pay even when the instance is idle
Spot instances = cheapest but CAN BE INTERRUPTED. Never use for uninterruptible critical real-time workloads
Reserved ≠ Savings Plans. RI = specific instance type + region binding. Savings Plans = flexible compute spend commitment
EBS volume is AZ-scoped — you CANNOT attach an EBS from AZ-1a to an EC2 in AZ-1b

STUDY_DATA.title: Storage Services
concepts: S3 (Object Storage): Bucket/key structure. HTTP(S) access. 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability. NOT a filesystem
S3 is Regional: Buckets are in specific Regions. S3 console appears global but each bucket has a home Region
EBS (Block Storage): Attached to ONE EC2 in SAME AZ. Like a virtual hard drive. Persistent after stop
EFS (Elastic File System): NFS v4 shared file storage. Multi-AZ. Auto-scales. Linux EC2 only
Instance Store: Physically attached to the host server. Highest I/O speed. EPHEMERAL — data LOST on stop/terminate
S3 Standard-IA: Infrequent access. Millisecond retrieval but retrieval FEE. 30-day minimum. 99.9% availability
Glacier Deep Archive: Cheapest S3 tier. 12-48 hr retrieval. 180-day minimum duration. Regulatory archival
Intelligent-Tiering: Auto-moves objects between tiers based on access patterns. Small monitoring fee. No retrieval fee
S3 durability: Data stored across minimum 3 AZs. 99.999999999% = losing 1 object per 10,000 years per 10M objects
traps: S3 is NOT a filesystem — you cannot mount it as a drive. Use EFS for shared file system access from EC2
EBS = Zonal resource. Cannot span AZs — if the AZ fails and EBS is not backed up, data is at risk
Instance Store data is LOST on instance stop (and terminate) — NEVER store important data here without backup
One Zone-IA = single AZ. If that AZ fails, data is LOST. Only for easily recreatable data

STUDY_DATA.title: Architecture & DR
concepts: High Availability (HA): Minimize downtime. Short automatic failover is acceptable. Multi-AZ is the key pattern
Fault Tolerance: Zero downtime, zero data loss. Requires FULL redundancy. More expensive than HA
DR Ladder 1 — Backup & Restore: Cheapest. Hours-level RTO/RPO. Cold data in S3 Glacier triggered after disaster
DR Ladder 2 — Pilot Light: Only CORE services (DB) always running. Everything else is off. Scale up after disaster. Minutes RTO
DR Ladder 3 — Warm Standby: Scaled-DOWN but fully functional copy running. Faster failover. More cost
DR Ladder 4 — Active-Active (Multi-Site): Zero downtime. Two full equal environments. Highest cost and complexity
RTO = Recovery Time Objective: MAX acceptable downtime after disaster. RPO = MAX acceptable data loss window
Multi-AZ pattern: Synchronous replication. Protects against AZ failure. RDS Multi-AZ = Standby (not readable)
Multi-Region pattern: Asynchronous replication. Protects against full Region failure. For global users
S3 built-in FT: Automatically stores objects across 3+ AZs. 11 nines durability. No extra config needed
traps: Multi-AZ does NOT protect against full Region failure. Multi-Region needed for true regional DR
RDS Multi-AZ Standby = NOT readable during normal operation. READ REPLICA = readable but async (not HA failover)
Pilot Light ≠ Warm Standby. Pilot Light = DB only running. Warm Standby = full env at reduced capacity
Active-Active = lowest RTO/RPO possible but HIGHEST cost and operational complexity

STUDY_DATA.title: Load Balancing & Auto Scaling
concepts: Elastic Load Balancer (ELB): AWS managed. Distributes traffic. Integrates with ASG for health checks + replacement
ALB (Application LB — Layer 7): HTTP/HTTPS only. Path-based (/api → service A), host-based, header-based routing
NLB (Network LB — Layer 4): TCP/UDP. Millions req/sec. Ultra-low latency. Supports static IP and Elastic IP
GLB (Gateway LB — Layer 3): Routes traffic through security virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) transparently
Auto Scaling Group (ASG): Automatically adds (scale out) or removes (scale in) EC2 instances based on demand
Target Tracking Scaling: Maintain a metric (e.g., CPU 50%). ASG auto-scales to hit and maintain the target
Step Scaling: Scale by defined increment steps when CloudWatch alarm thresholds are crossed
Scheduled Scaling: Pre-defined scale events (e.g., 09:00 Monday add 10 instances for known peak)
Loose Coupling: Components communicate via queues (SQS) or topics (SNS), not direct synchronous calls
traps: ALB = Layer 7 (Application/HTTP). NLB = Layer 4 (Transport/TCP). GLB = Layer 3 (Network). Do NOT confuse layers
Auto Scaling alone does NOT guarantee HA — you must deploy ASG ACROSS multiple AZs
ELB health checks + ASG = unhealthy instances are auto-terminated and replaced. This IS the HA pattern
Loose coupling pattern: use SQS between components to decouple. Direct synchronous calls = tight coupling (SPOF)

STUDY_DATA.title: Containers & Serverless
concepts: Lambda (FaaS): Event-triggered functions. Max 15min timeout. Max 10GB RAM. Pay per invocation count + duration (ms)
Lambda triggers: S3 events, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge, SNS, Cognito, etc.
ECS (Elastic Container Service): AWS-native container orchestration. Runs Docker containers on a cluster
ECS EC2 Launch Type: Containers run on EC2 instances YOU manage. More control, but more operational overhead
ECS Fargate Launch Type: Serverless containers. AWS manages underlying compute. Pay per task vCPU + RAM
EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service): Managed Kubernetes. Open-source compatible. Multi-cloud portable workloads
ECR (Elastic Container Registry): Managed Docker image registry. Store, version, scan, and manage container images
API Gateway: Create, publish, secure REST/HTTP/WebSocket APIs. Integrates with Lambda for serverless API pattern
Lambda vs Fargate: Lambda = short-lived function (event-driven, <15min). Fargate = long-running container service
traps: EC2 is NOT serverless. Lambda IS serverless. Fargate IS serverless for containers. ECS on EC2 is NOT serverless
Lambda has 15-minute MAX execution limit — not suitable for long-running batch jobs or persistent connections
EKS ≠ ECS. EKS uses Kubernetes (open-source). ECS uses AWS proprietary orchestration. Both run containers
API Gateway + Lambda = serverless API. This pattern has no servers to manage or pay for when idle

cloud.reason: Cloud fundamentals (CAPEX/OPEX, elasticity, agility) are the WHY behind every architectural decision and service model choice

infra.reason: The physical structure (Regions, AZs, Edge) directly determines what HA, DR, and latency patterns are possible

service-models.reason: IaaS/PaaS/SaaS determines the responsibility split, cost model, and what services you use for compute

deployment.reason: Whether you go Cloud, Hybrid, or On-Prem shapes your infrastructure layout and compliance responsibility

shared-responsibility.reason: Responsibility model changes per service type (IaaS vs PaaS) and deployment model (Cloud vs Hybrid)

ec2.reason: EC2 is the core IaaS compute unit — it connects to AZ placement, storage attachment, scaling groups, and security patching

storage.reason: Storage (S3/EBS/EFS) is consumed by compute (EC2/containers) and its durability/AZ scope determines DR strategy

architecture.reason: HA and DR patterns are built on top of Infrastructure (Regions/AZs), Compute (EC2), Storage, and Scaling services

scaling.reason: Load balancing and auto scaling are how you implement elasticity (a cloud advantage) and high availability in practice

containers.reason: Containers/Serverless are modern PaaS/FaaS implementations that replace EC2 management with managed compute models
